title: AstraZeneca — Hematology — Knowledge Doc
html:
Market Context: CALQUENCE in CLL/SLL and MCL

Product: CALQUENCE (acalabrutinib) Manufacturer: AstraZeneca Indications: Chronic lymphocytic leukemia/small lymphocytic lymphoma (CLL/SLL), mantle cell lymphoma (MCL) Document scope: Product-level competitive/clinical landscape (not wave-specific) Last updated: April 2026

Governing Thought

Calquence is fighting a two-front war: against Brukinsa for BTKi monotherapy/TTP dominance and against the fixed-duration paradigm shift driven by venetoclax-based combos. The AMPLIFY approval (Feb 2026) gives Calquence a unique bridge between these fronts — the first and only BTKi with both a continuous monotherapy AND a fixed-duration combo option — but execution on physician education and AMPLIFY awareness will determine whether this strategic advantage converts to sustained share leadership or erodes under Brukinsa's perception momentum and BeiGene's increasingly aggressive commercial machine.

1. Disease Landscape

1.1 Epidemiology

Chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) is the most common adult leukemia in Western countries. Small lymphocytic lymphoma (SLL) is the same disease with predominantly nodal rather than blood involvement.

  • New cases (US): ~20,000-21,000/year (CLL/SLL combined); ~1.2% of all new cancers
  • Prevalence: ~200,000+ living with CLL in the US (growing as survival improves with novel therapies)
  • Median age at diagnosis: ~70 years; predominantly affects older adults
  • 5-year survival rate: ~88% (dramatically improved from ~70% pre-BTKi era)
  • Incidence: More common in males (1.7:1 ratio), White Americans, and those with family history

Mantle cell lymphoma (MCL) is a rarer, more aggressive B-cell lymphoma:

  • New cases (US): ~4,000-5,000/year (~6% of all NHLs)
  • Median age at diagnosis: ~68 years; male predominance (3:1)
  • 5-year survival rate: ~50-55%; highly variable based on subtype (indolent vs blastoid)

1.2 Disease Biology

Key biological concepts relevant to the competitive landscape:

  • BTK (Bruton's tyrosine kinase): Essential kinase in B-cell receptor signaling pathway. Inhibition blocks survival, proliferation, and adhesion of malignant B cells. All covalent BTKis (acalabrutinib, zanubrutinib, ibrutinib) bind irreversibly to BTK at the C481 residue
  • BCL-2 (B-cell lymphoma 2): Anti-apoptotic protein overexpressed in CLL cells. Venetoclax (BCL-2 inhibitor) induces rapid apoptosis — mechanistically complementary to BTKi
  • C481S mutation: Acquired resistance mechanism to covalent BTKis. Drives interest in non-covalent BTKis (pirtobrutinib) for post-covalent-BTKi patients
  • Del(17p)/TP53 mutation: High-risk CLL markers associated with poor response to chemoimmunotherapy but preserved response to BTKi and venetoclax — a key differentiator for targeted therapies
  • IGHV mutation status: Unmutated IGHV predicts shorter time-to-first-treatment and poorer outcomes with CIT; particularly benefits from continuous BTKi or targeted therapy
  • Off-target kinase inhibition: The key differentiator among BTKis. Ibrutinib inhibits ITK, EGFR, TEC — driving cardiac toxicity (afib, hypertension). Acalabrutinib and zanubrutinib are more selective, with different off-target profiles that drive the safety narrative

1.3 Treatment Algorithm — CLL/SLL (2026)

Previously Untreated CLL/SLL (1L)
  |
  ├── Treat-to-Progression (TTP) Continuous Therapy:
  |     ├── CALQUENCE monotherapy or + obinutuzumab (ELEVATE-TN)
  |     ├── BRUKINSA monotherapy (SEQUOIA)
  |     └── Ibrutinib (declining — cardiac safety concerns)
  |
  ├── Fixed-Duration (FD):
  |     ├── CALQUENCE + venetoclax (AMPLIFY) — FDA approved Feb 2026 ★
  |     ├── Venetoclax + obinutuzumab (CLL14) — established standard
  |     └── [Pipeline: zanubrutinib + venetoclax (BOVen); zanubrutinib + sonrotoclax (CELESTIAL)]
  |
  v
Relapsed/Refractory CLL (2L+)
  |
  ├── BTKi-Naive:
  |     ├── CALQUENCE mono/combo, BRUKINSA mono/combo
  |     ├── Venetoclax + rituximab (MURANO)
  |     └── Pirtobrutinib emerging in BTKi-naive space (BRUIN CLL-313)
  |
  ├── BTKi-Experienced:
  |     ├── Venetoclax-based regimen (standard)
  |     ├── Pirtobrutinib (Jaypirca) — FDA approved for post-covalent-BTKi (Dec 2025)
  |     └── Clinical trials
  |
  v
Later Lines (3L+)
  |
  └── Pirtobrutinib (approved), venetoclax-based, clinical trials, CAR-T (emerging)

1.4 Treatment Algorithm — MCL (2026)

1L MCL
  |
  ├── Transplant-eligible:
  |     ├── CALQUENCE + BR (ECHO) — FDA approved Jan 2025 ★
  |     ├── High-dose chemo + ASCT
  |     └── R-CHOP or similar CIT +/- maintenance
  |
  ├── Transplant-ineligible:
  |     ├── BR, R-CHOP, VR-CAP
  |     ├── BTKi-based (CALQUENCE+BR per ECHO, ibrutinib + R per SHINE)
  |     └── [Pipeline: zanubrutinib-based combos]
  |
  v
R/R MCL (2L+)
  |
  ├── BTKi-naive: CALQUENCE, BRUKINSA, ibrutinib
  ├── BTKi-exposed: Pirtobrutinib (Jaypirca) — strongly preferred
  ├── CAR-T: brexucabtagene autoleucel (Tecartus)
  └── [Pipeline: sonrotoclax (BeOne), bispecifics]

1.5 Unmet Needs

  1. Fixed-duration options with BTKi efficacy: Patients and physicians want the efficacy of BTKi therapy without indefinite treatment. AMPLIFY addresses this directly — but awareness and adoption remain early
  2. BTKi resistance management: C481S and other resistance mutations after covalent BTKi therapy limit long-term efficacy. Pirtobrutinib addresses this but has limited data in earlier lines
  3. Cardiac safety in elderly CLL patients: Most CLL patients are >65 with cardiovascular comorbidities. BTKi cardiac effects (afib, hypertension) remain the #1 safety concern and the primary differentiator among agents
  4. High-risk CLL (del17p/TP53): These patients have worse outcomes even with targeted therapy. AMPLIFY excluded del17p/TP53 patients from its main analysis — a gap for Calquence
  5. MRD-guided treatment duration: Moving from fixed durations to MRD-guided stopping rules could optimize treatment length while preserving efficacy. True FD is currently preferred over MRD-guided (~43% vs ~27%)

2. Competitive Dynamics — The Only Battle That Matters

2.1 The Core Fight: Calquence vs Brukinsa

This is the 80/20. The entire competitive position of Calquence in CLL hinges on the Calquence-Brukinsa rivalry. Imbruvica is declining rapidly (revenue down ~17-20% YoY, patent expiring 2026, generics coming). Pirtobrutinib is a later-line niche player. The question is whether Calquence or Brukinsa becomes the default 2nd-gen BTKi.

Current Score (Q3 2025 ATU data, n=121):

Metric Calquence Brukinsa Edge
Preferred BTKi — Overall CLL Trails Leads Brukinsa
Preferred BTKi — 1L CLL (unweighted) 38% Higher Brukinsa
Preferred BTKi — 1L CLL (weighted by pt volume) Leads modestly Trails Calquence
Preferred BTKi — R/R BTKi Naive 24% Higher Brukinsa
Preferred BTKi — R/R BTKi Experienced Leads Trails Calquence
Best in Class Trails Leads Brukinsa
Most Efficacious Trails Leads Brukinsa
Safest Trails Leads slightly Brukinsa
Best Tolerated Trails Leads slightly Brukinsa
Ideal Combination Partner Leads Trails Calquence
Most Flexible Leads Trails Calquence
1L Forced Choice: PFS Trails Leads Brukinsa
1L Forced Choice: Overall Safety Trails Leads Brukinsa
1L Forced Choice: CV Risk Trails Leads Brukinsa
1L Forced Choice: Afib Risk Trails Leads Brukinsa
1L Forced Choice: Combo Options Leads Trails Calquence
1L Forced Choice: HTN Risk Leads Trails Calquence
1L Forced Choice: Neutropenia Risk Leads Trails Calquence
1L Forced Choice: Affordability Leads Trails Calquence

The Pattern: Brukinsa owns the "better single agent" narrative (efficacy, safety, tolerability, PFS, CV risk, afib). Calquence owns the "better platform" narrative (combination partner, flexibility, duration of treatment, combo options, affordability). This is the strategic lever for AZ: Calquence as the backbone of combinations, not just a monotherapy competitor.

High "Not Sure" Rates Are Critical: 25-31% of HCPs say "not sure" on forced choice attributes like PFS, afib, HTN, and CV risk. This is the battleground — the undecided HCPs who will determine which drug becomes the default.

2.2 Revenue Trajectory

Product FY2025 Revenue Growth US NRx Trend
Brukinsa $4.1B global ($2.95B US) +49% YoY >50% US new patient share across all CLL LoT
Calquence ~$3.5B+ global +13% YoY Leading 1L CLL new patient starts
Imbruvica ~$3.0B (declining) -17-20% YoY Rapid erosion, patent cliff 2026

Brukinsa has surpassed Calquence in quarterly revenue (Q3 2025: $1.0B vs $916M), though Calquence still claims leading 1L CLL new patient starts in the US. Brukinsa's growth is decelerating (from 2x to ~49% YoY) but absolute numbers are formidable.

2.3 Ibrutinib Decline (Context Only)

Imbruvica is not a competitive threat — it's a share donor. Key facts:

  • Revenue declining at ~17-20% YoY; projected to fall from ~$3.0B (2026) to ~$1.4B (2033)
  • Patent expiration in 2026 with generic entry expected
  • CMS price negotiations adding further downward pressure
  • Cardiac safety profile (afib, hypertension, sudden cardiac death) is the cautionary tale that benefits both Calquence and Brukinsa
  • Remaining ibrutinib patients are the easiest switch targets for both 2nd-gen BTKis

2.4 Pirtobrutinib (Jaypirca) — Later-Line Threat, Earlier-Line Watch

Pirtobrutinib is the first non-covalent (reversible) BTKi, offering a distinct mechanism:

  • Current approval (Dec 2025): R/R CLL/SLL after prior covalent BTKi — traditional approval based on BRUIN
  • BRUIN CLL-313 data: 80% risk reduction vs CIT in treatment-naive CLL — strong data for 1L expansion
  • BRUIN CLL-314: Met primary endpoint of non-inferiority on ORR vs ibrutinib in 1L+pretreated
  • ATU data (Q3 2025): Strongly preferred in R/R BTKi-experienced patients; physicians believe in its safety/tolerability advantage but less convinced of efficacy advantage in BTKi-naive settings
  • Implication for Calquence: If pirtobrutinib gains 1L approval, Calquence faces a three-way competition in frontline. However, the greater near-term threat is in R/R BTKi-naive where physicians already allocate increasing pirto share

2.5 BeOne/BeiGene Manufacturer Perception

This is an underappreciated competitive dimension:

  • AZ retains lead across most manufacturer performance metrics (robustness of studies, balanced data communication, partnership)
  • Warning signal: BeiGene/BeOne significantly improved on commitment to hematology, commitment to CLL, and innovative hematology pipeline
  • In MCL: BeOne has moved within top 3 on several metrics, surpassing AZ on some (pipeline, commitment)
  • BeOne rebranding from BeiGene signals long-term commitment to global hematology franchise

3. The AMPLIFY Inflection — Calquence's Strategic Pivot

3.1 What AMPLIFY Changes

AMPLIFY (Phase III, NEJM published) established Calquence + venetoclax (A+V) as the first all-oral, fixed-duration BTKi+BCL-2i combination in 1L CLL. FDA approved February 19, 2026.

Key Data:

  • 3-year PFS: 76.5% (A+V doublet), 83.1% (AVO triplet) vs 66.5% (CIT)
  • Superior PFS vs CIT with 10%+ absolute gain; median PFS not reached for A+V
  • Low afib (0.7%), low hypertension (4.1%), low major hemorrhage (1%), low treatment discontinuation due to ARs (8%) with A+V
  • 89% of A+V patients did not require subsequent treatment at 3 years
  • ~94% 3-year OS rate with A+V

Treatment regimen: 14 cycles (28 days each) — acalabrutinib starts cycle 1, venetoclax added at cycle 3 with ramp-up. All oral, no infusions needed.

3.2 Pre-Launch Awareness & Adoption Readiness (as of Q3 2025)

KPI Q4 '24 Q1 '25 Q2 '25 Q3 '25 Trend
AMPLIFY familiarity (T3B) 67% 71% 73% 76% Growing
AMPLIFY familiarity (T2B) 42% 42% 50% 50% Flat
BTKi+BCL2i can work through complementary mechanisms (T2B) 71% 61% 61% 72% Recovering
Comfort using BTKi+BCL2i upfront vs sequentially (T2B) 51% 52% 51% 60% Growing
Likely to prescribe BTKi+BCL2i post-FDA (T2B) 46% 41% 42% 51% Growing
Stated future CAL 1L CLL share (next 20 pts) 23% 27% 26% 29% Growing

Critical insight: AMPLIFY awareness is growing but deep familiarity (T2B) is only 50%. Two-fifths of oncologists remain unsure of the PFS and OS data. The launch requires aggressive medical education, not just awareness.

3.3 Barriers to A+V Adoption (Mar 2026 ATU)

Top barriers (among physicians not increasing A+V usage):

  1. Efficacy concerns: OS data (not yet mature), PFS not dramatically superior to Ven+O
  2. Tolerability concerns: Discontinuation rates and GI symptoms
  3. Treatment duration: Some prefer true TTP over fixed-duration
  4. Experience/confidence: Lack of personal experience with the combination

Top drivers (among physicians increasing A+V usage):

  1. Efficacy: PFS and OS data from AMPLIFY
  2. Treatment duration: Preference for fixed-duration, all-oral regimen
  3. Safety: Low cardiovascular AE rates
  4. Clinical guidelines: NCCN inclusion

3.4 The FD vs TTP Tension

The CLL market is splitting along a philosophical divide:

  • ~20-25% of physicians often/always prefer fixed-duration regimens for standard-risk patients
  • Only ~5% often/always prefer FD for high-risk patients
  • ~43% somewhat or highly prefer true fixed-duration over MRD-guided treatment
  • ~27% somewhat or highly prefer MRD-guided treatment

Calquence's unique position: it can play BOTH sides — continuous monotherapy/TTP (ELEVATE-TN) AND fixed-duration combo (AMPLIFY). No other BTKi has this duality.

4. MCL Landscape — ECHO Changes the Game

4.1 ECHO Approval Impact

ECHO (Phase III) established Calquence + bendamustine + rituximab (BR) as the first and only BTKi-based regimen approved in 1L MCL. FDA approved January 2025.

Key data:

  • 27% risk reduction in PFS (CAL+BR vs placebo+BR)
  • mPFS: 5.5 years with CAL+BR vs 4.1 years with placebo+BR
  • ORR: 91% CAL+BR vs 88% placebo+BR
  • CR: 67% CAL+BR vs 54% placebo+BR

4.2 MCL Competitive Position (Q3 2025, n=50)

  • 1L MCL: Calquence most preferred BTKi overall (48%, down from 53% in Q2)
  • R/R BTKi Naive: Zanubrutinib most preferred
  • R/R BTKi Experienced: Pirtobrutinib strongly preferred
  • 1L MCL share: BTKi class has strong prescribing (~50%+), with Calquence commanding just over half of BTKi share, predominantly as CAL+BR
  • Manufacturer perception: AZ still leads on robustness of studies and partnership, but BeOne improved significantly on several metrics

4.3 MCL Challenges

  • Sample sizes are small (n=50 in Q3) — volatile quarter-to-quarter
  • ECHO awareness plateauing at 48% (T3B); needs reinvigoration per ATU team
  • Zanubrutinib gaining in multiple combination forms (zanu+R, zanu mono)
  • Pirtobrutinib dominates R/R BTKi-experienced — limits Calquence lifecycle in MCL

5. Messaging & Promotional Landscape

5.1 Calquence CLL Key Messages (Q1 2026 Digital Tracker)

Top recalled messages (among HCPs interacting with Calquence reps, Mar 2026):

  1. CAL41: ~84% and ~76% 6-year OS rates with CAL+obinutuzumab and CAL monotherapy (40% recall)
  2. CAL32: CALQUENCE + venetoclax is first/only all-oral FD regimen in 1L CLL (37%)
  3. CAL33: CALQUENCE is first/only BTKi recommended by NCCN (37%)
  4. CAL35: Consistent PFS in del(17p)/TP53m patients (37%)
  5. CAL37: ~94% 3-year OS rate with CAL+venetoclax (37%)

Message effectiveness leaders (motivating + believable + differentiating):

  • CAL40 (6-year PFS): 70% effectiveness
  • CAL1 (90% PFS risk reduction vs GClb): 70% effectiveness
  • CAL38 (89% no subsequent treatment at 3 years): 65% effectiveness

Brukinsa counter-messages:

  • BRU1: Superior PFS vs ibrutinib (ALPINE) — highest impact competitive message
  • BRU15: MAIC analysis suggested improved PFS and CR vs Calquence — direct competitive claim

5.2 Share of Voice

  • Calquence CLL rep reach (All MDs, Mar 2026): 48% (trending up from 31% in Apr 2025)
  • Brukinsa CLL rep reach (All MDs, Mar 2026): 39% (volatile, was 47% in Jun 2025)
  • Jaypirca CLL rep reach (All MDs, Mar 2026): 56% (surged in Q4 2025)
  • Calquence frequency remains highest among all brands

6. Pipeline & Future Threats

6.1 Near-Term (2026-2027)

Agent Sponsor Trial Threat Level
Pirtobrutinib 1L CLL Lilly BRUIN CLL-313 High — 80% risk reduction vs CIT; likely 1L FDA submission
Zanubrutinib + venetoclax BeOne BOVen Medium — 1L CLL combination directly competitive to A+V
Zanubrutinib + sonrotoclax BeOne CELESTIAL-TNCLL High — next-gen BCL-2i could leapfrog venetoclax combos

6.2 Medium-Term (2027-2029)

  • Sonrotoclax: BeOne's next-gen BCL-2 inhibitor, more selective than venetoclax, overcomes BCL2 G101V resistance mutation. Phase 3 in 1L CLL (CELESTIAL) and R/R MCL. Breakthrough therapy designation in R/R MCL. If zanubrutinib + sonrotoclax proves superior to venetoclax + obinutuzumab, BeOne could own the entire CLL toolkit (best BTKi + best BCL-2i)
  • Pirtobrutinib combinations: BTKi + BCL-2i combos with pirtobrutinib backbone could emerge
  • Non-covalent BTKi in earlier lines: If BRUIN CLL-313 leads to 1L pirtobrutinib approval, the BTKi market becomes a three-player race

6.3 The Existential Question for Calquence

Can Calquence maintain relevance if:

  1. Brukinsa is perceived as the better monotherapy BTKi
  2. Pirtobrutinib captures the post-BTKi-failure market AND moves into 1L
  3. Sonrotoclax makes venetoclax-based combos obsolete (undermining AMPLIFY's partner drug)

The answer depends entirely on whether AMPLIFY converts Calquence from "one of three BTKis" to "the BTKi designed for combinations." That narrative must succeed.

7. ZoomRx Work & Measurement Framework

7.1 Active Studies

Study Type Frequency Key Focus
CLL ATU Attitude, Trial, Usage Bi-quarterly (~6 weeks) BTKi preference, AMPLIFY KPIs, forced choice, share
MCL ATU Attitude, Trial, Usage Bi-quarterly (reduced to quarterly H2 2025) ECHO launch tracking, BTKi preference, R/R dynamics
Digital Tracker (CAL KPI) Message recall & effectiveness Quarterly Rep reach, SOV, message impact, competitive messaging

7.2 2026 ATU Revamp (Critical Context)

AZ is redesigning the CLL ATU for the post-AMPLIFY world (Feb 2026 discussion document):

Key changes:

  • Primary focus shifting to 1L and actionability
  • Survey shortened to <25 minutes for better completion
  • New forced choice exercises: FD vs TTP, A+V vs V+O, A mono vs Z mono
  • New patient-type segmentation: standard risk fit, standard risk unfit, high risk fit, high risk unfit, moderate risk (unmutated IGHV) fit
  • Drivers & barriers algorithm: always ask for A+V, plus 2 other products with anticipated change
  • Mini-PET integrated (message recall/NPP within ATU)
  • Target: N=150/quarter, target list HCPs, ~30% academic

Implication for ZoomRx: This revamp is a significant scope change. The survey redesign is an opportunity to upsell design consulting, ensure ZoomRx retains the study, and position for additional ad-hoc studies (e.g., AMPLIFY launch tracking, community vs academic segmentation).

8. Key Data Sources

  • CALQUENCE CLL ATU Q3 2025 Report (Oct 2025, Luminas LLC)
  • CALQUENCE MCL ATU Q3 2025 Report (Oct 2025, Luminas LLC)
  • CAL KPI Digital Tracker (through Q1 2026)
  • CLL ATU Revamp Discussion Document (Feb 2026)
  • AstraZeneca FY2025 earnings announcement
  • FDA approval announcement: acalabrutinib + venetoclax for CLL/SLL (Feb 19, 2026)
  • AMPLIFY Phase III: NEJM 2025 publication
  • ECHO Phase III data
  • BeiGene/BeOne FY2025 earnings
  • Lilly pirtobrutinib press releases (BRUIN CLL-313, CLL-314)

The AMPLIFY approval (Feb 2026) gives Calquence a unique bridge between these fronts — the first and only BTKi with both a continuous monotherapy AND a fixed-duration combo option — but execution on physician education and AMPLIFY awareness will determine whether this strategic advantage converts to sustained share leadership or erodes under Brukinsa's perception momentum and BeiGene's increasingly aggressive commercial machine.

The Pattern: Brukinsa owns the "better single agent" narrative (efficacy, safety, tolerability, PFS, CV risk, afib). Calquence owns the "better platform" narrative (combination partner, flexibility, duration of treatment, combo options, affordability). This is the strategic lever for AZ: Calquence as the backbone of combinations, not just a monotherapy competitor.
High "Not Sure" Rates Are Critical: 25-31% of HCPs say "not sure" on forced choice attributes like PFS, afib, HTN, and CV risk. This is the battleground — the undecided HCPs who will determine which drug becomes the default.

Brukinsa has surpassed Calquence in quarterly revenue (Q3 2025: $1.0B vs $916M), though Calquence still claims leading 1L CLL new patient starts in the US. Brukinsa's growth is decelerating (from 2x to ~49% YoY) but absolute numbers are formidable.
2.3 Ibrutinib Decline (Context Only)
Imbruvica is not a competitive threat — it's a share donor. Key facts:

Revenue declining at ~17-20% YoY; projected to fall from ~$3.0B (2026) to ~$1.4B (2033)
Patent expiration in 2026 with generic entry expected
CMS price negotiations adding further downward pressure
Cardiac safety profile (afib, hypertension, sudden cardiac death) is the cautionary tale that benefits both Calquence and Brukinsa
Remaining ibrutinib patients are the easiest switch targets for both 2nd-gen BTKis

2.4 Pirtobrutinib (Jaypirca) — Later-Line Threat, Earlier-Line Watch
Pirtobrutinib is the first non-covalent (reversible) BTKi, offering a distinct mechanism:

Current approval (Dec 2025): R/R CLL/SLL after prior covalent BTKi — traditional approval based on BRUIN
BRUIN CLL-313 data: 80% risk reduction vs CIT in treatment-naive CLL — strong data for 1L expansion
BRUIN CLL-314: Met primary endpoint of non-inferiority on ORR vs ibrutinib in 1L+pretreated
ATU data (Q3 2025): Strongly preferred in R/R BTKi-experienced patients; physicians believe in its safety/tolerability advantage but less convinced of efficacy advantage in BTKi-naive settings
Implication for Calquence: If pirtobrutinib gains 1L approval, Calquence faces a three-way competition in frontline. However, the greater near-term threat is in R/R BTKi-naive where physicians already allocate increasing pirto share

2.5 BeOne/BeiGene Manufacturer Perception
This is an underappreciated competitive dimension:

AZ retains lead across most manufacturer performance metrics (robustness of studies, balanced data communication, partnership)
Warning signal: BeiGene/BeOne significantly improved on commitment to hematology, commitment to CLL, and innovative hematology pipeline
In MCL: BeOne has moved within top 3 on several metrics, surpassing AZ on some (pipeline, commitment)
BeOne rebranding from BeiGene signals long-term commitment to global hematology franchise


3. The AMPLIFY Inflection — Calquence's Strategic Pivot
3.1 What AMPLIFY Changes
AMPLIFY (Phase III, NEJM published) established Calquence + venetoclax (A+V) as the first all-oral, fixed-duration BTKi+BCL-2i combination in 1L CLL. FDA approved February 19, 2026.
Key Data:

3-year PFS: 76.5% (A+V doublet), 83.1% (AVO triplet) vs 66.5% (CIT)
Superior PFS vs CIT with 10%+ absolute gain; median PFS not reached for A+V
Low afib (0.7%), low hypertension (4.1%), low major hemorrhage (1%), low treatment discontinuation due to ARs (8%) with A+V
89% of A+V patients did not require subsequent treatment at 3 years
~94% 3-year OS rate with A+V

Treatment regimen: 14 cycles (28 days each) — acalabrutinib starts cycle 1, venetoclax added at cycle 3 with ramp-up. All oral, no infusions needed.

Critical insight: AMPLIFY awareness is growing but deep familiarity (T2B) is only 50%. Two-fifths of oncologists remain unsure of the PFS and OS data. The launch requires aggressive medical education, not just awareness.
3.3 Barriers to A+V Adoption (Mar 2026 ATU)
Top barriers (among physicians not increasing A+V usage):

Efficacy concerns: OS data (not yet mature), PFS not dramatically superior to Ven+O
Tolerability concerns: Discontinuation rates and GI symptoms
Treatment duration: Some prefer true TTP over fixed-duration
Experience/confidence: Lack of personal experience with the combination

Top drivers (among physicians increasing A+V usage):

Efficacy: PFS and OS data from AMPLIFY
Treatment duration: Preference for fixed-duration, all-oral regimen
Safety: Low cardiovascular AE rates
Clinical guidelines: NCCN inclusion

3.4 The FD vs TTP Tension
The CLL market is splitting along a philosophical divide:

~20-25% of physicians often/always prefer fixed-duration regimens for standard-risk patients
Only ~5% often/always prefer FD for high-risk patients
~43% somewhat or highly prefer true fixed-duration over MRD-guided treatment
~27% somewhat or highly prefer MRD-guided treatment

Calquence's unique position: it can play BOTH sides — continuous monotherapy/TTP (ELEVATE-TN) AND fixed-duration combo (AMPLIFY). No other BTKi has this duality.

4. MCL Landscape — ECHO Changes the Game
4.1 ECHO Approval Impact
ECHO (Phase III) established Calquence + bendamustine + rituximab (BR) as the first and only BTKi-based regimen approved in 1L MCL. FDA approved January 2025.
Key data:

27% risk reduction in PFS (CAL+BR vs placebo+BR)
mPFS: 5.5 years with CAL+BR vs 4.1 years with placebo+BR
ORR: 91% CAL+BR vs 88% placebo+BR
CR: 67% CAL+BR vs 54% placebo+BR

4.2 MCL Competitive Position (Q3 2025, n=50)

1L MCL: Calquence most preferred BTKi overall (48%, down from 53% in Q2)
R/R BTKi Naive: Zanubrutinib most preferred
R/R BTKi Experienced: Pirtobrutinib strongly preferred
1L MCL share: BTKi class has strong prescribing (~50%+), with Calquence commanding just over half of BTKi share, predominantly as CAL+BR
Manufacturer perception: AZ still leads on robustness of studies and partnership, but BeOne improved significantly on several metrics

4.3 MCL Challenges

Sample sizes are small (n=50 in Q3) — volatile quarter-to-quarter
ECHO awareness plateauing at 48% (T3B); needs reinvigoration per ATU team
Zanubrutinib gaining in multiple combination forms (zanu+R, zanu mono)
Pirtobrutinib dominates R/R BTKi-experienced — limits Calquence lifecycle in MCL


5. Messaging & Promotional Landscape
5.1 Calquence CLL Key Messages (Q1 2026 Digital Tracker)
Top recalled messages (among HCPs interacting with Calquence reps, Mar 2026):

CAL41: ~84% and ~76% 6-year OS rates with CAL+obinutuzumab and CAL monotherapy (40% recall)
CAL32: CALQUENCE + venetoclax is first/only all-oral FD regimen in 1L CLL (37%)
CAL33: CALQUENCE is first/only BTKi recommended by NCCN (37%)
CAL35: Consistent PFS in del(17p)/TP53m patients (37%)
CAL37: ~94% 3-year OS rate with CAL+venetoclax (37%)

Message effectiveness leaders (motivating + believable + differentiating):

CAL40 (6-year PFS): 70% effectiveness
CAL1 (90% PFS risk reduction vs GClb): 70% effectiveness
CAL38 (89% no subsequent treatment at 3 years): 65% effectiveness

Brukinsa counter-messages:

BRU1: Superior PFS vs ibrutinib (ALPINE) — highest impact competitive message
BRU15: MAIC analysis suggested improved PFS and CR vs Calquence — direct competitive claim

5.2 Share of Voice

Calquence CLL rep reach (All MDs, Mar 2026): 48% (trending up from 31% in Apr 2025)
Brukinsa CLL rep reach (All MDs, Mar 2026): 39% (volatile, was 47% in Jun 2025)
Jaypirca CLL rep reach (All MDs, Mar 2026): 56% (surged in Q4 2025)
Calquence frequency remains highest among all brands

6.2 Medium-Term (2027-2029)

Sonrotoclax: BeOne's next-gen BCL-2 inhibitor, more selective than venetoclax, overcomes BCL2 G101V resistance mutation. Phase 3 in 1L CLL (CELESTIAL) and R/R MCL. Breakthrough therapy designation in R/R MCL. If zanubrutinib + sonrotoclax proves superior to venetoclax + obinutuzumab, BeOne could own the entire CLL toolkit (best BTKi + best BCL-2i)
Pirtobrutinib combinations: BTKi + BCL-2i combos with pirtobrutinib backbone could emerge
Non-covalent BTKi in earlier lines: If BRUIN CLL-313 leads to 1L pirtobrutinib approval, the BTKi market becomes a three-player race

6.3 The Existential Question for Calquence
Can Calquence maintain relevance if:

Brukinsa is perceived as the better monotherapy BTKi
Pirtobrutinib captures the post-BTKi-failure market AND moves into 1L
Sonrotoclax makes venetoclax-based combos obsolete (undermining AMPLIFY's partner drug)

The answer depends entirely on whether AMPLIFY converts Calquence from "one of three BTKis" to "the BTKi designed for combinations." That narrative must succeed.

7.2 2026 ATU Revamp (Critical Context)
AZ is redesigning the CLL ATU for the post-AMPLIFY world (Feb 2026 discussion document):
Key changes:

Primary focus shifting to 1L and actionability
Survey shortened to <25 minutes for better completion
New forced choice exercises: FD vs TTP, A+V vs V+O, A mono vs Z mono
New patient-type segmentation: standard risk fit, standard risk unfit, high risk fit, high risk unfit, moderate risk (unmutated IGHV) fit
Drivers & barriers algorithm: always ask for A+V, plus 2 other products with anticipated change
Mini-PET integrated (message recall/NPP within ATU)
Target: N=150/quarter, target list HCPs, ~30% academic

Implication for ZoomRx: This revamp is a significant scope change. The survey redesign is an opportunity to upsell design consulting, ensure ZoomRx retains the study, and position for additional ad-hoc studies (e.g., AMPLIFY launch tracking, community vs academic segmentation).
